planting.note: Plant bare-root or container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; European black elderberry is the classic elderflower and elderberry species of English country-garden lore — grown for the intensely fragrant cream-white flower clusters used in elderflower cordial and champagne, and for the drooping clusters of small, jet-black berries used in elderberry syrup, wine, and preserves.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; prune hard in late winter to maintain shape and vigor. Sambucus nigra (black elderberry or European elder) is the classic European elder — a fast-growing, multi-stemmed deciduous shrub or small tree long cultivated for its remarkable botanical generosity: in late spring it produces large, flat-topped, highly fragrant clusters (corymbs) of tiny, cream-white flowers with a distinctive musky-lychee-pear elderflower fragrance; by late summer these transform into heavy, drooping clusters of small (1/4-inch), jet-black, glistening berries. Both flowers and berries are culinary staples in English and Scandinavian cooking: elderflower cordial (steeped flowers, sugar, lemon, citric acid) is one of England's most beloved summer drinks; elderflower 'champagne' (a lightly fermented drink) and elderflower fritters are summer traditions. The berries are made into elderberry syrup (rich in anthocyanins and vitamin C, widely sold as an immune-support supplement), elderberry wine, elderberry jam, and in Scandinavian juices (hyldebær saft). CAUTION: Raw flowers and berries contain mild cyanogenic glycosides — cook berries before eating in quantity; flower clusters are generally safe raw in cordials and wine but not in large raw amounts. The cultivar 'Sambuco' and 'Haschberg' are selected for large berry yields; 'Black Beauty' and 'Black Lace' are ornamental dark-leaved forms.
sources: University of Minnesota Extension — Elderberry Production
National Center for Home Food Preservation — Elderberry

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to part shade; rose cactus (Pereskia bleo) is a primitive, leafy, climbing cactus from Colombia and Panama — the most un-cactus-like cactus, producing true, broad, succulent-edged leaves and large, rose-like orange flowers, with the leaves used as a vegetable in Malaysia and the Philippines where the plant has naturalized.

directions: Plant rooted stem cuttings in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; provide support for climbing stems. Pereskia bleo (rose cactus or wax rose) is a Colombian and Panamanian climbing cactus that challenges every expectation of what a cactus looks like: it produces true, broad, elliptical, dark-green leaves (2–4 inches long) on stout, armed (spiny) stems — not the water-storing leafless paddles of familiar cacti. Pereskia species are the most evolutionarily 'primitive' of all cacti, representing the ancestral form from which leafless cacti evolved by gradually reducing their leaves while enlarging their stems as water storage organs. Rose cactus produces attractive, 2-inch, bright-orange to salmon-orange, multi-petaled flowers resembling wild roses or thin-petaled hibiscus, followed by fleshy, pear-shaped, orange-yellow fruits. In Malaysia, Indonesia, and the Philippines — where the plant has naturalized — the leaves are eaten as a vegetable: the thick, slightly succulent, mildly flavored leaves are blanched, stir-fried, or added to soups. In traditional medicine across Southeast Asia, the leaves are used topically for skin conditions and as an anti-inflammatory preparation. A fascinating specimen plant for tropical collectors and those interested in the botanical history of cacti.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Pereskia
University of Malaya — Tropical Edible Cacti

common_name: Bushy Lippia (White Lippia, Lemon Verbena Relative)

planting.note: Plant rooted stem cuttings in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to part shade; Lippia alba is a South American and Caribbean aromatic shrub with lemon-mint to spearmint scented leaves — it is one of the most widely used medicinal herbs in Brazil (cidreira) and throughout Latin America, used as a calming tea for anxiety, insomnia, and digestive upset.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; drought-tolerant once established. Lippia alba (bushy lippia or cidreira in Brazil) is a sprawling, aromatic, semi-woody perennial shrub from South America and the Caribbean, native from Mexico south to Argentina, that has become one of the most widely used and important medicinal herb teas throughout Latin America. The leaves have a pleasant, complex aroma ranging from lemon-mint to spearmint-citrus to slightly camphor-herbaceous depending on the chemotype (several distinct essential oil chemotypes exist: carvone-type, linalool-type, and citral-type, each with different scent profiles). In Brazil, where it is called 'erva-cidreira' or 'falsa melissa,' it is the most popular folk remedy for anxiety, nervous tension, insomnia, and digestive upset — sold fresh in markets and as a prepared tea in pharmacies. Throughout South and Central America, the leaves are used as a flavoring herb for desserts, drinks, and savory dishes. As a garden plant in zone 9–12, Lippia alba is ornamental, drought-tolerant, and virtually pest-free. The small, pale-lavender to white flowers attract pollinators. It is distinct from lemon verbena (Aloysia citrodora, formerly Lippia citriodora) but closely related and in the same family (Verbenaceae).
sources: Brazilian Ministry of Health — Medicinal Plants — Lippia alba
Purdue University Center for New Crops — Lippia alba
CABI Invasive Species Compendium — Lippia alba

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in early spring (or fresh in fall) in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, moderately fertile mountain-type soil in full sun to part shade; baldmoney is a Scottish and Alpine European mountain herb of the carrot family with feathery, intensely aromatic foliage combining anise, caraway, and lovage flavors — it requires cool growing conditions and does not perform in hot lowland climates.

directions: Direct sow fresh seed in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, cool, moderately fertile soil; fresh seed germinates best — old seed has very poor germination. Meum athamanticum (baldmoney or meu) is a rare and unusual European mountain herb of the carrot family (Apiaceae), native to the highlands of Scotland, Wales, and the Alps, where it grows on moist, acidic mountain meadows and rocky slopes at high elevations. The intensely aromatic, finely cut, ferny, bright-green foliage has a distinctive, complex fragrance and flavor combining anise, caraway, dill, celery, and lovage — more aromatic and complex than any of those herbs individually. The roots (called 'bear root' — bears reportedly seek out and dig the roots in spring) are also aromatic and have been used in alpine herbal medicine and liqueur production. In Scottish Gaelic folk medicine and cooking, the leaves were used to flavor soups and stews in the Highlands where few other herbs grew. The plant produces white umbel flowers in early summer, resembling a small cow parsley. As a garden plant, baldmoney requires cool, moist, acidic conditions found in zone 4–7 mountain and cool-temperate climates — it does not tolerate summer heat and is genuinely rare in cultivation, making it a prized collector's plant for cool-climate herb enthusiasts.
sources: Royal Botanic Garden Edinburgh — Scottish Native Herbs
Plants for a Future Database — Meum athamanticum

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; Mundu is a Philippine and Southeast Asian Garcinia species producing golf-ball-sized, round, golden-yellow fruits with sweet-tart, apricot-mango-flavored flesh — less complex than its famous relative mangosteen but easier to grow and more productive in tropical home gardens.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; Garcinia dulcis is slow-growing and prefers consistent moisture. Garcinia dulcis (mundu or sweet mangosteen) is a tropical fruit tree native to the Philippines, Indonesia, and Malaysia — a member of the mangosteen family (Clusiaceae) and related to the famous mangosteen (G. mangostana) but distinctly different in fruit character. Mundu produces round, golf-ball-to-tennis-ball-sized fruits with smooth, thin, golden-yellow to orange-yellow skin and bright-yellow, juicy, mildly sweet-tart flesh with a flavor profile combining apricot, mango, and a slight citrus note — pleasant, refreshing, and more accessible in flavor than mangosteen's complex floral-tropical character. The yellow latex (gamboge) in the skin and pith is mildly bitter and slightly resinous (as in all Garcinia species) — peel carefully and avoid including the skin in the edible flesh. Mundu is significantly easier to grow than true mangosteen, which is notoriously difficult and slow; mundu is faster-maturing, more forgiving of less-than-perfect conditions, and more productive. The tree is also useful as a rootstock for grafting true mangosteen in regions where mangosteen is difficult to grow on its own roots. In the Philippines, the fruit is eaten fresh and made into jam; the large seeds contain a fat used in traditional cosmetics.
sources: Purdue University Center for New Crops — Garcinia dulcis
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Garcinia Species

common_name: Yaw Weed (Wild Indian Mulberry, Cheese Shrub)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy coastal soil in full sun to part shade; yaw weed is a native Florida, Caribbean, and Gulf Coast coastal shrub related to noni (Morinda citrifolia) that grows on sandy beaches, shell middens, and disturbed coastal areas — it is an important native host plant for several butterfly species and produces unusual, compound white fruits similar in structure to noni.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy soil in full sun to part shade; drought and salt-spray tolerant once established. Morinda royoc (yaw weed or wild Indian mulberry) is a native North American coastal shrub in the coffee family (Rubiaceae) — related to noni (Morinda citrifolia) and producing very similar compound, syncarpous white fruits (multiple small flowers fuse into a single fleshy structure), though much smaller and with a less objectionable odor than commercial noni. It is native to the coastal hammocks, shell middens, sandy beaches, and disturbed coastal areas of South Florida, the Florida Keys, the Caribbean islands, and the coastal Gulf of Mexico. The plant has a distinctly scrambling or climbing growth habit, with the stems leaning on and scrambling through other shrubs. The roots yield a traditional reddish-orange dye used by Caribbean indigenous peoples; the leaves and bark have traditional medicinal uses in Caribbean herbal medicine for skin conditions and fevers — the 'yaw weed' name refers to use for yaws (a tropical bacterial infection). As a native coastal landscape plant, yaw weed is exceptionally salt, drought, and wind tolerant — suitable for difficult coastal conditions where little else grows. The fruits are edible but not palatable fresh; birds eat them readily, providing wildlife habitat value.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Native Coastal Plants
